
::: {.paper-table}
**Table 35. C.8 Economics, nudges, and campaign persuasion**

| Finding | Published effect | Implication if true | Source |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
| Academic nudge studies (social comparison, defaults, framing) | 8.7 percentage points average | Low-cost behavioral interventions produce substantial real-world changes when deployed at population scale | DellaVigna & Linos [@dellavigna2022] — academic sample |
| Microcredit as poverty reduction | Substantial positive effects | Small loans are a general-purpose anti-poverty instrument at population scale | 1990s observational and small-sample reports |
| Door-to-door canvassing on vote choice | Several pp per contact in early papers | A small number of well-targeted doorstep conversations could decide a national election | Pre-Kalla-Broockman literature |
| Contact hypothesis on prejudice | d large in early work | Brief positive cross-group contact substantially reduces prejudice long-term | Paluck & Green lit reviews reveal more mixed evidence |
| LaCour canvassing on gay marriage attitudes | d very large, durable | A single 20-minute conversation permanently shifts political attitudes on polarized issues | LaCour & Green [@lacour2014] — retracted 2015 for fabricated data |
| Financial literacy education → behavior | substantial in early work | Classroom financial education substantially improves real-world financial decisions | Fernandes, Lynch & Netemeyer [@fernandes2014] collapse this to r ≈ 0.04 |
| CEO effects on firm performance | reported large | Hiring the right CEO substantially determines shareholder returns | Bertrand & Schoar find small effects; most variance is firm and industry |
| Hurricane-name matching on charitable giving | d reported large | Sharing a first letter with a hurricane’s name substantially raises your probability of donating | Chandler et al. [@chandler2008] |
| Name-letter effect (Dennis → dentist) | substantial | People are meaningfully more likely to choose careers and spouses sharing letters with their own name | Pelham et al. [@pelham2002] series |
:::
